title: Waling weave
subtitle: The three-rod lock weave for basket borders and transitions
excerpt: A three-rod weave worked simultaneously in a chasing sequence that locks basket elements together at transitions: outer base edge, above staking-up rows, and below the border. More rigid than randing, waling is the structural backbone of willow basketry.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for waling construction in willow basketry. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on basketry technique and construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A three-rod willow weave in which rods chase each other in sequence, producing a rigid band that locks basket elements in place.
In basketry, a single-rod over-and-under weave for building height or filling an area. Less rigid than waling.

content.text: Waling is the weave used to hold a basket together at its critical points: the outer edge of the base, just after staking up, and just before the border. Three rods work simultaneously in a chasing sequence that locks every stake in place. A
band is denser and more rigid than
and resists the basket opening or collapsing at transitions.

Insert three rods butt-first behind three consecutive stakes. Each rod is threaded behind its own stake and points to the right. The leftmost rod goes in front of the next two stakes, behind the third, and out to the right. Then the middle rod does the same. Then the rightmost rod. This establishes the chasing sequence.

Always take the leftmost of the three active rod ends, weave it in front of two stakes and behind one, then pull it snug against the previous row. The rod that was second becomes the new leftmost, and you repeat. Use the rapping iron to press each completed pass down firmly before taking the next. The pattern is always: over two, under one, left rod first.

When you return to the starting point, the three finishing rods need to pass through the first three passes to close the circuit. Use the bodkin to open a path through the start rods and thread each finishing rod in the correct over-two-under-one sequence. The join must follow the same rhythm as the rest of the row or it will show as a loose spot.
Weaving over only one stake: the standard waling pattern is over two, under one. Over-one-under-one gives a randing row, not a waling row, and does not lock the basket.
Picking up rods out of order: always pick up the leftmost active rod end first. Taking rods in the wrong order produces an irregular weave that will not close correctly.

Closing the circuit without the bodkin: threading the last three rod ends through the start without opening a clear path results in the rods crossing each other under the start rows. Open each path individually with the bodkin before threading.
